# Devlog 35: Retrieval Quality Audit for Episodic Memory (2026-03-18)

*Generated by research-copilot*

## Overview

Gate check for I05 (episodic memory: similar-problem retrieval with worked solutions). Before building any pipeline, we need to know: can embedding-based retrieval find structurally similar math problems? If nearest-neighbor retrieval returns topically unrelated problems, the entire approach collapses regardless of how good the injected solutions are.

**Method:** TF-IDF with bigrams over problem text. 20 Omni-MATH queries (d3-d7, seed=42). Two retrieval banks: Math L5 (2,156 competition problems) and Omni-MATH self-retrieval (4,428 problems, leave-one-out).

## Results Summary

| Retrieval Bank | Mean top-1 sim | Median | Min | Max |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Math L5 → Omni query | 0.330 | 0.283 | 0.194 | 0.757 |
| Omni self (leave-one-out) | 0.485 | 0.450 | 0.141 | 0.871 |

Self-retrieval within Omni-MATH is substantially better than cross-corpus retrieval from Math L5. The retrieval bank must cover the same difficulty range as the eval problems.

## Qualitative Assessment

### Good matches (structurally similar, sim > 0.5)

| Query | Retrieved | Sim | Assessment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dice probability (d3.5) | Another dice probability | 0.871 | Same approach domain, combinatorial counting |
| Recurrence a_n+1 (d4.5) | Similar recurrences | 0.819 | Same technique family (characteristic eq, generating functions) |
| Recurrence a_n+1 (d5.0) | Nearly identical recurrence | 0.772 | Structurally excellent match |
| Functional equation (d5.0) | Other functional equations | 0.704 | Same problem class, similar proof strategies |
| Infinite series (d6.0) | Other infinite series | 0.596 | Same manipulation techniques |

These are the cases where episodic memory would be most useful — a worked solution to a structurally similar problem provides direct reasoning scaffolding.

### Mediocre matches (topically related, 0.3-0.5)

| Query | Retrieved | Sim | Assessment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Base-3 digit property (d4.0) | Base-2 digit counting | 0.499 | Same theme, different approach |
| Game theory on grid (d5.0) | Other grid games | 0.480 | Same setup, different strategies |
| Triangle reflections (d5.0) | Points in complex plane | 0.507 | Geometry but different sub-field |

TF-IDF captures topic but not approach. Neural embeddings might improve these.

### Poor matches (surface only, sim < 0.2)

| Query | Retrieved | Sim | Assessment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Optimization on rays (d6.0) | Triangle cevians | 0.141 | Completely different |

Proof-based and constructive problems have the weakest retrieval. These are the hardest problems (d6+) where concepts already fail most (-17pp at d7-8).

## Key Finding: Problem Type Determines Retrieval Quality

| Problem Type | Typical Sim | Retrieval Quality | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Recurrences/sequences | 0.7-0.8 | Excellent | Formulaic structure, shared vocabulary |
| Functional equations | 0.6-0.7 | Good | Distinctive syntax pattern |
| Probability (specific objects) | 0.5-0.9 | Good | Object names match well (dice, marbles) |
| Infinite series/sums | 0.5-0.6 | Good | Notation and structure match |
| Combinatorics (counting) | 0.3-0.5 | Mediocre | Topic matches but approach varies |
| Geometry | 0.2-0.4 | Poor | Same objects (triangles) but wildly different techniques |
| Constructive/proof | 0.1-0.2 | Poor | Unique problem structure, no shared vocabulary |

## Implications for I05

**Positive signals:**
1. For ~50% of problem types (recurrences, functional equations, series, probability), TF-IDF finds structurally similar problems with high confidence (sim > 0.5)
2. Self-retrieval within the same corpus is substantially better than cross-corpus — the bank must match the eval difficulty
3. The well-matched problem types are also those where seeing a worked solution is most likely to help (shared techniques)

**Concerns:**
1. For geometry and constructive problems, retrieval quality is too low for useful worked examples
2. The hardest problems (d6+) have the worst retrieval — these are exactly where we'd most want help
3. TF-IDF is purely surface-level; neural embeddings should improve the 0.3-0.5 range

**Recommendation:** Proceed to smoketest, but with realistic expectations:
- Episodic memory will likely help on formulaic problem types (recurrences, functional equations, series)
- It will likely be neutral on geometry and constructive problems
- Net effect depends on the distribution of problem types in the eval set
- Neural embeddings are a likely next step if TF-IDF-based smoketest shows promise

## Next Step

Design a smoketest: 20 Omni-MATH problems, retrieve top-1 from rest of corpus, inject the full solution as context, compare to baseline. Use the self-retrieval bank (not Math L5). Run with Qwen3.5-Flash via OpenRouter.
